slides.title: ACME Extension for Public Key Challenges (pk-01)
SM2 Dual Certificates on ACME: From Bootstrap Issuance to STAR-Based Renewal

polls.text: Should we start another Call for Adoption for the pk-01 draft?

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: We did not ask for note-takers.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: Still need a minute-taker volunteer

chat.author: Michael Richardson
chat.text:
Hi, if you wonder when rfc8366bis (cluster 484) will progress: rfc8366bis, is through many directorate reviews, through AD review, and awaits IESG DISCUSS.
I'm in ATP.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Thanks to Peter Liu for helping with the notes.

chat.author: Esko Dijk
chat.text: Adding to the "cluster 484 delay" story: the IESG telechat is now planned for 2026-08-06.  So the 157-week wait should be coming to a conclusion ...

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: @Tim: I'm less opposed to standardizing something that the CA/BF would then need to adapt to: at worst we'd end up in a situation where there's Method 23 "Persistent DNS" and a separate Method 24 "Persistent DNS - ACME", and frankly I kinda like that situation. Having DNS-01 rolled into Method 7 is frequently somewhat annoying.

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: What I was pointing out that we want to avoid is a Method 24 that has to change anything in the RFC, i.e. "Implement RFC  XXXX with the following modifications ..."

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: Ah gotcha, yes that's definitely worth avoiding.

chat.author: Shiloh Heurich
chat.text: https://github.com/cabforum/servercert/issues/677 is intended to be the "use acme + dns-persist" method

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: I would prefer to see it adopted cleanly, which is why I welcome the author's efforts to coordinate and not diverge

chat.author: Michael Slaughter
chat.text: Agreed Tim. That is the intent :)

chat.author: Henry Birge-Lee
chat.text: regarding support for key rollover, the key after the initial validation (in which it thwarts the ACME proxy attack) is not actively serving a security purpose. the true security binding is with the account URL contained in the hash. Even if the hash was made with a compromised key, assuming the key was not compromised at the time the record was made, it still shows the domain owners intent to have the record persist with that account url (regardless of how many times the account owner rolls the key)

chat.author: Henry Birge-Lee
chat.text: I will put a more full-flushed security analysis on github

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: ty henry

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: @Henry: For sure. However, if support for that sort of key rollover is required, then the CA will be required to maintain storage of historical keys associated with accounts. I'd prefer to not be required to do that, at the expense of being slightly less user-friendly when evaluating dns-persist-01 challenge attempts. My preference would be that the document discuss support of historical keys and why doing so is acceptable, but not require that the CA do so.

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: I don't have an opinion yet, which is why I would appreciate Henry's writeup. I might have one after reading the proposal and writeup :)

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: I agree with aaron that non-essential extensions should be optional.

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: There still is value in stating the right way to do it, if there are good reasons to do it. I'm agnostic on that ATM.

chat.author: Henry Birge-Lee
chat.text: Thanks Tim and Aaron. I will put a write up and then we can continue discussion.

chat.author: Henry Birge-Lee
chat.text: In a prior draft we did have somewhat aggressive requirements for CAs keeping historical keys but the most robust proposal would require the CA to store at most 1 historical key hash per a domain validated per an ACME account and only keep this key if the participant is using it actively in DNS persist records.

chat.author: Antonios Chariton
chat.text: WiFi (IEEE) is interested

chat.author: Jan Klaußner
chat.text: I think its needed, also if we think about KEM based auth

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: I love how the transcriber transcribed "faces"  being cheated off with rsa...

chat.author: John Gray
chat.text: Yes, we need KEM keys for SMIME and KEM auth, or even just CMS file encryption...

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: +1 to Yoav: keep it in a single draft, and keep the ACME WG apprised of the situation

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: please use the mic!

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: Agreed, happy with the status quo of the changes being in PLANTS with occasional cross-pollination to the ACME list and meetings.

chat.author: Tadahiko Ito
chat.text: I could not get what information is needed to  protect against harvest attack during ACME protocol execution?

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Tadahiko Ito said:

I could not get what information is needed to  protect against harvest attack during ACME protocol execution?

Do you want to ask that at the mic?

chat.author: Tadahiko Ito
chat.text: sure..

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: MTIs come back to bite us once again.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: I like Aaron's suggestion. Nice and simple.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: :plus:

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: :plus::plus:

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: I would suggest not defining "profiles", if nothing else to avoid being confused with ACME profiles.

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: oh that's a good point

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: Agreed. I think the simpler document I proposed wouldn't be complex enough to be tempted to introduce the word "profiles".

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: draft-ietf-acme-delete-one-sentence

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: draft-ietf-acme-that-mti-did-not-age-well

chat.author: Andrew Chen
chat.text: This reinforces that sharing customer domains on a single cert can cause that single cert be revoked if any domain chooses to revoke it.

chat.author: Michael Slaughter
chat.text: Is there a gap between this proposed mechanisms and what CAA records with the accounturi extension already provides?

chat.author: Andrew Chen
chat.text: Prevent issuance in the first place -- isn't that what the CA account binding is for?

chat.author: Tadahiko Ito
chat.text: this proposal revoke previous certs?

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: The attacker will know more about this method existing

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: than the original owner will

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: you will be teaching the poor sod how to fix their account

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: while the attacker is murdering everyone

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: To Tim's point: what if two legitimate ACME clients for the same domain automatically do the "Revoke Other" thing, and go to war over it?

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: Bob's point is what I'm worried about

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: A normal user just loses to an attacker by being less sophisticated

chat.author: Feng Geng
chat.text: Even with CAA, My intuition is that the new account still needs to provide some form of proof to establish a security linkage to the prior account.

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: No need for the new account to prove that it is the rightful successor of the previous account. After all, the previous account could be the legitimate one or it could be the attacker's account -- no way for the CA to tell the difference. The new account proves is right simply by being able to complete validations.

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: Right, if you can update a CAA record and complete DCV, you've actually basically proved control of the domain twice already ...

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: And by putting the account in CAA, you're binding it going forward

chat.author: Tim Hollebeek
chat.text: It's actually quite elegant

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Did the authors read RFC 2804 and RFC1984?
